title: Cropping & Resizing
concept: Cropping cuts out a region of an image and discards the rest, while resizing keeps the whole scene but resamples it to new overall dimensions.

exercises.prompt: Recap: When you scale an image up or down, what happens to its content?

options.text: Every pixel stays but moves closer to or farther from a center point — nothing is discarded
Half the pixels are permanently deleted
The image is converted to a different file format

exercises.explain: Right — scaling repositions pixels but keeps all of the content. Cropping and resizing behave very differently.

cards.title: Two very different editing buttons
cards.body: Open any photo app and you'll usually see two separate tools: "Crop" and "Resize." They sound similar, and both change an image's dimensions — but one throws pixels away forever, and the other keeps every part of the photo, just at a different resolution.

cards.title: Cropping vs. resizing
cards.body: Cropping cuts out a rectangular region and permanently discards everything outside it — the output shows less of the original scene. Resizing keeps the entire scene but resamples it onto a grid with more or fewer pixels, stretching or shrinking the whole image to new overall dimensions.

cards.title: Scissors vs. a photocopier zoom
cards.body: Cropping is like cutting a photo with scissors — whatever's outside the cut line is gone for good. Resizing is like using a photocopier's zoom control — the whole page shrinks or enlarges together, but every part of the original page is still represented, just at a different scale.

cards.title: Feeding photos into a neural network
cards.body: Most vision models expect a fixed input size, like 224×224 pixels. A photo pipeline often crops first — trimming to the subject and dropping distracting background — then resizes that crop to exactly 224×224 so every image, no matter its original shape, fits the network's expected input.

cards.title: Resizing isn't the same as scaling from before
cards.body: It's easy to lump resizing in with the scaling from the last lesson, but there's a subtlety: scaling (as introduced earlier) moves pixels within the same canvas and can leave empty space or spill outside the frame. Resizing changes the canvas's actual pixel dimensions and resamples the full image to fit the new grid — the entire picture is always kept, just redrawn at a new resolution.

cards.title: What survives the operation
cards.body: The core difference: does content get thrown away, or just redrawn?

left.text:
Keeps a sub-region
Everything outside is discarded

right.text:
Keeps the whole scene
Resampled to new pixel dimensions

exercises.prompt: You cut a photo down to just the top-left corner and discard the rest. Which operation is this?
exercises.explain: Correct — permanently discarding everything outside a chosen region is exactly what cropping does.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Resizing an image to a smaller resolution still represents the entire original scene, just with fewer pixels.
exercises.explain: True. Resizing resamples the whole image onto a new grid — nothing is cut away, it's just redrawn at a different pixel density.

exercises.sentence: Unlike resizing, cropping permanently ___ the pixels outside the chosen region.
exercises.explain: Discards is correct — cropping removes everything outside the selected rectangle, while resizing keeps all content.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario describes resizing rather than cropping?

options.text: A 4000×3000 photo is shrunk down to 224×224 while keeping the full scene visible
A photo of a crowd is trimmed to show only one person's face, discarding the rest
A photo is cut in half, keeping only the left side

exercises.explain: Shrinking the whole image while keeping every part of the scene visible is resizing, not cropping.

exercises.prompt: A dataset pipeline crops each product photo tightly around the item, then resizes every crop to the same 128×128 size before training. Why use both steps instead of just one?

options.text: Cropping removes irrelevant background so the model focuses on the product, and resizing then standardizes every image to the same dimensions the network requires
Cropping and resizing do the exact same thing, so using both is redundant
Resizing alone would delete the background just as effectively as cropping
Cropping is only used to change file format, not image content

exercises.explain: Exactly — crop to focus on what matters, resize to fit the model's required input shape. Next: transforms that combine translation, rotation, scaling, and one more move — shearing — into a single operation.
